units.name: Period 1: 1491–1607
decks: Native American Societies Before European Contact
Columbian Exchange, Spanish Exploration, and Conquest
Labor, Slavery, and Caste in the Spanish Colonial System
The Americas and Europe Before Contact
units.objectives:
PERIOD 1: 1491–1607 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 4–6% of exam)

KC-1.1: Before European contact, Native Americans developed diverse societies across North America.
- Varied cultures: settled agricultural societies (Pueblo, Mississippian mound builders) and nomadic hunter-gatherer societies.
- Complex political, religious, and economic structures. Trade networks spanning the continent.

KC-1.2: European exploration was motivated by desire for wealth, glory, and spread of Christianity.
- Spanish conquest: Reconquista mindset, encomienda system, forced labor. Columbus 1492, Cortés/Aztec 1519, Pizarro/Inca 1532.
- Portuguese, Spanish, French, Dutch, and English pursued different colonization strategies.

KC-1.3: The Columbian Exchange transformed both hemispheres.
- Europe to Americas: horses, cattle, pigs, wheat, smallpox, measles, influenza → catastrophic indigenous population decline (up to 90%).
- Americas to Europe: corn, potatoes, tomatoes, tobacco, cacao → population growth in Europe.
- Demographic collapse of Native Americans reshaped social, labor, and political systems in the Americas.

KC-1.3.II: Spanish established labor systems using indigenous and African populations.
- Encomienda → repartimiento → African slavery as native populations declined.
- Caste system (limpieza de sangre) organized Spanish colonial society.
- Missions spread Catholicism; often coercive. Pueblo Revolt 1680 demonstrates resistance.

units.name: Period 2: 1607–1754
decks: Transatlantic Trade and the Atlantic World
units.objectives:
PERIOD 2: 1607–1754 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 6–8% of exam)

KC-2.1: European colonization created distinct regional cultures in British North America.
- New England: Puritan settlement (Plymouth 1620, Massachusetts Bay 1630), covenant community, town meetings, subsistence farming. Halfway Covenant.
- Chesapeake: Virginia (Jamestown 1607), tobacco economy, indentured servants → African slavery, Bacon's Rebellion 1676. Maryland — Catholic refuge.
- Middle Colonies: New York, Pennsylvania (Quakers), diverse religious tolerance, mixed economy.
- Southern Colonies: rice/indigo economy in Carolinas, plantation slavery, slave codes.

KC-2.2: The Atlantic world created economic and cultural ties between Europe, Africa, and the Americas.
- Triangular trade: manufactured goods → Africa, enslaved Africans → Americas, raw materials → Europe.
- Mercantilism: Navigation Acts restricted colonial trade to benefit England.
- Salutary neglect: Britain's relaxed enforcement allowed colonial self-governance to develop.

KC-2.3: The growth of slavery transformed colonial society.
- Middle Passage: brutal transatlantic slave trade. 1619 — first Africans in Virginia.
- Slave codes formalized racial slavery: slaves as property, hereditary status.
- Stono Rebellion (1739). African cultural retentions (Gullah language/culture).

KC-2.3.III: Colonial societies developed relative autonomy.
- Colonial assemblies exercised taxing and legislative powers. House of Burgesses (1619).
- Great Awakening (1730s–1740s): emotional revivalism, challenged hierarchies, increased religious pluralism. Jonathan Edwards, George Whitefield.
- Enlightenment ideas (Locke, natural rights) began influencing colonial political thought.

units.name: Period 3: 1754–1800
decks: The Seven Years' War (French and Indian War)
Philosophical Foundations of the American Revolution
Diplomacy and Foreign Policy in the Early Republic
units.objectives:
PERIOD 3: 1754–1800 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-3.1: The Seven Years' War (French and Indian War, 1754–1763) transformed colonial relationships.
- Proclamation of 1763 banned settlement west of Appalachians — colonists angered.
- British war debt → new taxation policy ending salutary neglect.

KC-3.2: Colonial protest escalated toward revolution.
- Stamp Act (1765), Townshend Acts (1767), Tea Act (1773), Intolerable Acts (1774).
- Continental Congress, Sons of Liberty, Boston Massacre (1770), Boston Tea Party (1773).
- Ideological foundations: Locke's natural rights, Montesquieu's separation of powers, 'no taxation without representation.'

KC-3.2.II: Revolutionary ideals had varied impacts.
- Declaration of Independence (1776): 'all men are created equal'; slavery contradicted ideals.
- Women: Republican Motherhood. Abigail Adams 'Remember the Ladies.'
- Loyalists (Tories) fled to Canada/Britain. Native Americans lost British protection.

KC-3.3: The new nation struggled with governance under the Articles of Confederation.
- Articles: no taxation power, no executive, weak central government. Northwest Ordinance (1787).
- Shays' Rebellion (1786–87) revealed weakness of federal government.

KC-3.3.II: The Constitution created a stronger federal government.
- Constitutional Convention 1787: Great Compromise (bicameral), 3/5 Compromise, Electoral College.
- Federalists (Hamilton, Madison, Jay — Federalist Papers) vs. Anti-Federalists (Patrick Henry).
- Bill of Rights (1791) addressed Anti-Federalist concerns.

KC-3.3.III: Early republic established precedents.
- Washington's Farewell Address: avoid entangling alliances, warn against factions.
- Hamilton's financial program: national bank, assumption of state debts, tariffs → controversy.
- Whiskey Rebellion (1794). XYZ Affair. Alien and Sedition Acts (1798). Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions (nullification).

units.name: Period 4: 1800–1848
decks: The Second Great Awakening and Reform Movements
units.objectives:
PERIOD 4: 1800–1848 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-4.1: Democratic ideals expanded political participation.
- Revolution of 1800: Jefferson's election peacefully transferred power between parties.
- Expansion of white male suffrage (property requirements dropped). Rise of political parties.
- Jacksonian Democracy: appeal to 'common man.' Spoils system (patronage). Indian Removal Act (1830) → Trail of Tears.
- Nullification Crisis (1832): South Carolina — states' rights vs. federal authority.

KC-4.2: The Market Revolution transformed the American economy.
- Transportation revolution: National Road, Erie Canal (1825), railroads. Connected markets.
- Factory system: Lowell Mill girls, early industrialization in Northeast. Shift from artisan production.
- Expansion of market economy; growing regional economic specialization.

KC-4.2.II: Regional economies diverged.
- North: wage labor, industrialization. South: plantation slavery, cotton ('King Cotton'). West: subsistence and commercial farming.
- Cotton gin (1793) intensified slavery in the South. Domestic slave trade expanded westward.

KC-4.3: Reform movements challenged existing social order.
- Second Great Awakening: revivalism, perfectionism, social reform impulse. Charles Finney.
- Abolitionism: Garrison (immediate abolition — The Liberator), Douglass. Underground Railroad.
- Women's rights: Seneca Falls Convention (1848), Declaration of Sentiments. Sojourner Truth.
- Temperance, public education (Horace Mann), prison reform (Dorothea Dix), utopian communities.

KC-4.3.III: Territorial expansion intensified sectional conflict.
- Louisiana Purchase (1803), War of 1812, Florida cession (1819), Oregon Treaty (1846), Mexican-American War (1846–48) → Mexican Cession.
- Missouri Compromise (1820): Missouri slave, Maine free, 36°30' line.
- Manifest Destiny ideology justified westward expansion; displaced Native Americans and Mexicans.

units.name: Period 5: 1844–1877
decks: Manifest Destiny and the Mexican-American War
Sectional Crisis and the Compromise of 1850
Slavery Debates and the Kansas-Nebraska Act
units.objectives:
PERIOD 5: 1844–1877 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-5.2: Intensifying slavery debates led to sectional crisis.
- Wilmot Proviso (1846): proposed banning slavery in territory from Mexico. Compromise of 1850: California free, popular sovereignty in New Mexico/Utah, Fugitive Slave Act strengthened.
- Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854): popular sovereignty repealed Missouri Compromise → 'Bleeding Kansas' → formation of Republican Party.
- Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857): enslaved people not citizens, Congress cannot ban slavery in territories.
- Lincoln-Douglas debates (1858). John Brown's raid on Harper's Ferry (1859).

KC-5.3: Southern secession and the Civil War.
- Lincoln's election (1860) → South Carolina secession → Confederacy formed.
- Fort Sumter (April 1861) begins war. Border states remained in Union.
- Military strategy: Anaconda Plan (Union), Confederate defense. Key battles: Antietam, Gettysburg, Vicksburg.
- Emancipation Proclamation (Jan. 1, 1863): freed slaves in Confederate states; redefined war aims. 54th Massachusetts (USCT).
- 13th Amendment (1865) abolished slavery.

KC-5.3.II: Reconstruction attempted to reintegrate Southern states and protect freedpeople.
- Presidential Reconstruction (Lincoln/Johnson): lenient, quick readmission.
- Congressional (Radical) Reconstruction: 14th Amendment (citizenship, equal protection), 15th Amendment (Black male suffrage), Freedmen's Bureau, military occupation.
- Black political participation: African Americans elected to Congress and state offices.

KC-5.3.III: Reconstruction ended with the Compromise of 1877.
- Rise of KKK and white supremacist violence. Enforcement Acts (1870–71) then abandoned.
- Sharecropping replaced slavery economically. Black Codes → Jim Crow laws.
- Compromise of 1877: Hayes presidency, federal troops withdrawn from South. End of Reconstruction.

units.name: Period 6: 1865–1898
decks: The Transcontinental Railroad and Economic Development
units.objectives:
PERIOD 6: 1865–1898 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-6.1: Westward expansion transformed the West and dispossessed Native Americans.
- Homestead Act (1862): encouraged settlement. Transcontinental Railroad (1869) accelerated settlement.
- Plains Wars: Little Bighorn (1876), Wounded Knee (1890). Dawes Act (1887): allotment, assimilation, reduced tribal land.
- Closing of the frontier: Turner's Frontier Thesis (1893).

KC-6.2: Industrialization transformed the American economy.
- Railroad expansion: integrated national market. Captains of industry / robber barons: Carnegie (steel), Rockefeller (oil), Morgan (finance).
- Vertical and horizontal integration. Social Darwinism justified inequality. Gospel of Wealth (Carnegie).
- Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) — largely ineffective initially.

KC-6.3: Immigration and urbanization reshaped American society.
- Old immigration (Northern/Western Europe) → New immigration (Southern/Eastern Europe, China). Nativist backlash: Chinese Exclusion Act (1882), nativism.
- Tenements, political machines (Tammany Hall). Hull House (Jane Addams) — settlement house movement.

KC-6.3.II: Labor organized to challenge industrial capitalism.
- Knights of Labor: industrial unionism; declined after Haymarket Affair (1886).
- American Federation of Labor (AFL, Samuel Gompers): craft unions, collective bargaining.
- Homestead Strike (1892), Pullman Strike (1894). Government sided with business (injunctions).

KC-6.3.III: Agrarian protest challenged economic inequality.
- Grange → Farmers' Alliances → Populist (People's) Party. Omaha Platform (1892): graduated income tax, direct election of senators, railroad regulation, silver coinage.
- Election of 1896: Bryan vs. McKinley. Populism absorbed by Democratic Party; largely defeated.

units.name: Period 7: 1890–1945
decks: World War I and American Neutrality
The Road to World War II
units.objectives:
PERIOD 7: 1890–1945 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-7.1: America emerged as a global power through imperialism.
- Spanish-American War (1898): Cuba independence, acquisition of Philippines, Puerto Rico, Guam. Anti-Imperialist League (Mark Twain) vs. expansionists (Mahan, Roosevelt).
- Open Door Policy in China. Roosevelt Corollary to Monroe Doctrine. Panama Canal (1914).

KC-7.2: Progressivism addressed industrial-age social problems.
- Muckrakers: Upton Sinclair (The Jungle), Ida Tarbell (Standard Oil), Jacob Riis (How the Other Half Lives).
- Reforms: initiative/referendum/recall, direct election of senators (17th Amendment, 1913), income tax (16th Amendment, 1913), women's suffrage (19th Amendment, 1920).
- Roosevelt's Square Deal: trust-busting, conservation. Wilson's New Freedom: Federal Reserve, Clayton Antitrust Act. NAACP (1909), Booker T. Washington vs. W.E.B. Du Bois.

KC-7.3: WWI and aftermath shaped American society.
- Neutrality → Zimmermann Telegram, unrestricted submarine warfare → US entry (1917).
- Espionage and Sedition Acts curtailed civil liberties. Great Migration: African Americans moved north.
- Wilson's 14 Points; Treaty of Versailles; Senate rejected League of Nations (isolationism).
- 1920s: Red Scare, Palmer Raids, Sacco and Vanzetti. Harlem Renaissance. Prohibition. Fundamentalism vs. Modernism (Scopes Trial 1925).

KC-7.2.III: Great Depression and New Deal.
- Stock market crash (1929). Causes: overproduction, credit/speculation, weak banking, tariffs (Smoot-Hawley). Hoover's limited response.
- FDR's New Deal: 3 Rs (Relief, Recovery, Reform). Key programs: CCC, AAA, FDIC, Social Security Act (1935), Wagner Act. Critics: Huey Long, Father Coughlin, Supreme Court challenges.
- New Deal coalition: urban workers, African Americans, labor unions, ethnic minorities.

KC-7.3.III: WWII.
- Appeasement, Munich (1938), rise of fascism. US isolationism: Neutrality Acts. Lend-Lease.
- Pearl Harbor (Dec. 7, 1941) → US entry. Home front: war production, rationing, women in workforce (Rosie the Riveter), Japanese American internment (Korematsu v. US).
- D-Day (1944), island-hopping in Pacific, Manhattan Project. Atomic bombs → Japan surrender (1945).

units.name: Period 8: 1945–1980
decks: Postwar Prosperity and the Baby Boom
units.objectives:
PERIOD 8: 1945–1980 — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 10–17% of exam)

KC-8.1: The Cold War shaped American foreign and domestic policy.
- Containment (Kennan), Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan, NATO. Berlin Airlift (1948–49). NSC-68: massive military buildup.
- Korean War (1950–53): limited war, ended in stalemate. Eisenhower's 'massive retaliation,' military-industrial complex warning.
- McCarthyism, Second Red Scare, HUAC, Hollywood Ten, Rosenbergs. Loyalty oaths, blacklists.

KC-8.2: Postwar prosperity reshaped American society.
- GI Bill, suburban growth (Levittown), baby boom, rise of car culture. Television transformed culture.
- Conformity of 1950s vs. beats/counterculture. Civil religion, 'under God' in pledge.

KC-8.3: Civil Rights Movement challenged segregation.
- Brown v. Board of Education (1954). Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–56, MLK). Sit-ins (1960), Freedom Rides (1961). March on Washington, 'I Have a Dream' (1963).
- Civil Rights Act (1964), Voting Rights Act (1965). Malcolm X, Black Power, Black Panther Party.
- Fair Housing Act (1968). Assassination of MLK. Kerner Commission: 'two societies.'

KC-8.3.II: Johnson's Great Society expanded federal role.
- Medicare/Medicaid, Elementary and Secondary Education Act, Voting Rights Act. War on Poverty (OEO).

KC-8.3.III: Vietnam War divided the nation.
- Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) escalated US involvement. Tet Offensive (1968) eroded public support.
- Anti-war movement: Kent State (1970). Nixon's Vietnamization. War Powers Act (1973). Fall of Saigon (1975).

KC-8.3.IV: Social movements transformed American society.
- Feminist movement: NOW (1966), Title IX, Roe v. Wade (1973). Chicano, American Indian, LGBTQ movements.
- Nixon: détente with China and USSR, SALT I. Watergate scandal and resignation (1974). Ford's pardon.

units.name: Period 9: 1980–Present
decks: The End of the Cold War
Economic and Social Change in the 1980s–90s
September 11 and the War on Terror
units.objectives:
PERIOD 9: 1980–Present — AP US History CED Learning Objectives (approx. 4–6% of exam)

KC-9.1: Conservative politics reshaped domestic and foreign policy.
- Reagan Revolution: supply-side economics (Reaganomics), tax cuts, deregulation, reduction of federal programs. Religious Right, Moral Majority, social conservatism.
- Iran-Contra Affair. Reagan Doctrine: support anti-communist movements. SDI ('Star Wars').

KC-9.2: The Cold War ended.
- Gorbachev's glasnost and perestroika. Fall of Berlin Wall (1989). Soviet dissolution (1991).
- Bush Sr.: Gulf War (1990–91). 'New World Order.'

KC-9.3: Clinton era: economic boom and political polarization.
- NAFTA (1993), budget surplus, dot-com boom. Welfare reform (1996). Impeachment (1998).
- Culture wars: debates over multiculturalism, affirmative action, LGBTQ rights.

KC-9.3.II: Post-9/11 foreign policy.
- September 11, 2001. USA PATRIOT Act, Department of Homeland Security. War in Afghanistan (2001).
- Iraq War (2003): WMD justification. Abu Ghraib, debate over civil liberties vs. security.

KC-9.3.III: 21st century challenges.
- Hurricane Katrina (2005): government failure. Financial crisis (2008). Obama election: historic significance.
- Globalization: outsourcing, immigration debates. Tea Party movement, political polarization.
- ACA (Affordable Care Act, 2010). Income inequality. #MeToo, BLM movements.
